task: I have a workflow with a dozen <Task> nodes and I want a single token budget to cover all of them, but I do NOT want to add a budget prop to every individual Task. How does Smithers' <Aspects> get its tokenBudget/latencySlo down to all those descendant Tasks without me touching each one? Explain the mechanism.
notes: A correct answer explains that <Aspects> wraps a subtree and propagates its config DOWN THE TREE via React context (AspectContext): descendant Task components read the context value, so the scope-wide budget applies to every descendant without editing each Task. The engine enforces the propagated budget at task-dispatch time. Must convey 'wrap the subtree, config flows to descendants automatically through context', not 'set a prop on each Task'. Wrong if it claims you must annotate every Task or that Aspects only attaches to its direct children.

task: A run wrapped in <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 50_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }}> blew past 50k tokens and the run failed. What is the exact error CODE Smithers raised when the budget was exceeded? Answer with just the code.
notes: From docs: onExceeded 'fail' raises ASPECT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED with payload { kind, limit, current }.

task: Author a Smithers workflow for a long iterative agent loop that MUST be capped at 80,000 total tokens. Wrap the whole thing so a token-budget breach HARD-FAILS the run (so a durable catch/clear can react). Use an <Aspects> with tokenBudget max 80000 and onExceeded 'fail' around a <Sequence> that holds a <Loop> running an agent <Task>; after the loop a final <Task> summarizes. Wire ids and a Zod output schema for each task.
canonicalAnswer: <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 80_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }}> wrapping a <Sequence> that contains a <Loop> of an agent <Task> plus a trailing summary <Task>
notes: Render check requires <Aspects>, <Sequence>, <Loop>, <Task>; the workflow must render via smithers graph. costBudget/<Budget do not exist.

task: On <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 200_000, perTask: 20_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }}> one of my tasks clearly burned ~45k tokens but the run never failed at that task. The run only failed later, once the cumulative total crossed 200k. Is perTask broken, or am I misunderstanding it?
notes: Correct answer: tokenBudget.perTask is NOT enforced yet (the docs explicitly say 'not enforced yet'). Only the scope-wide max (cumulative run token total) is enforced, at task-dispatch time. So a single task exceeding perTask=20_000 does nothing; the run fails only when the accumulated total crosses max. This is expected behavior, not a bug. Wrong if it claims perTask currently caps individual tasks.

task: Author a deploy-promotion workflow whose canary checks must finish within a 2-minute wall-clock SLO. Run several canary probe agent <Task>s in <Parallel>, enforce a scope-wide latency SLO of 120000 ms that FAILS the run if exceeded, then an <Approval> gate to promote. Use <Aspects> with latencySlo, plus <Parallel>, <Task>, and <Approval>, with ids and Zod output schemas.
canonicalAnswer: <Aspects latencySlo={{ maxMs: 120_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }}> wrapping a <Parallel> of canary <Task> probes, followed by an <Approval> promotion gate
notes: Render check requires <Aspects>, <Parallel>, <Task>, <Approval>. latencySlo onExceeded supports only fail|warn (NOT skip-remaining); costBudget does not exist.

task: There is exactly one onExceeded value that tokenBudget accepts but latencySlo does NOT. Name it.
notes: tokenBudget onExceeded: fail|warn|skip-remaining; latencySlo onExceeded: fail|warn. skip-remaining is token-only.

task: Two questions about <Aspects latencySlo>: (1) does latencySlo.perTask put a wall-clock timeout on each individual task, and (2) is latencySlo.maxMs measured per-task or from the start of the whole run?
notes: Correct: (1) latencySlo.perTask is NOT enforced yet (documented), so it gives no per-task timeout today. (2) latencySlo.maxMs is the scope-wide wall-clock budget measured from the RUN'S START (cumulative elapsed since the run started), checked at task-dispatch before each descendant task, not a per-task cutoff. Wrong if it claims perTask enforces a per-task timeout or that maxMs resets/measures per task.

task: I'm wrapping a long fan-out in <Aspects tokenBudget>. When the budget is hit I do NOT want the whole run to error out; I just want Smithers to stop dispatching the remaining tasks and let the run finish what it already produced. Which onExceeded value do I pick, and how is it different from 'warn' and 'fail'?
notes: Correct: onExceeded:'skip-remaining' — once the budget is exceeded the about-to-dispatch task is skipped (remaining descendant tasks don't run) and the run continues/finishes instead of erroring. Contrast: 'fail' raises ASPECT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED and fails the run; 'warn' just logs and keeps running everything anyway (no budget cut-off). Must pick skip-remaining AND correctly contrast all three.

task: The Aspects runtime accumulator (createAccumulator) keeps running totals within a scope. Name its three numeric counter fields exactly.
notes: From packages/components/src/aspects/AspectAccumulator.ts: { totalTokens, totalLatencyMs, taskCount }. There is no cost counter.

task: Outer <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 100_000, onExceeded: 'warn' }}>. Nested inside it, <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 20_000 }}> where I left onExceeded off. For tasks inside the INNER scope, what max and what onExceeded apply — does the inner inherit the outer's onExceeded 'warn'?
notes: Correct: nested <Aspects> replace a budget field WHOLESALE, not per-field. Because the inner sets tokenBudget at all, the entire inner tokenBudget object replaces the outer's for inner-scope tasks: max becomes 20_000 and onExceeded falls back to its DEFAULT 'fail' (NOT the inherited 'warn'). Only the tracking config (tokens/latency) merges per-field from the parent. Must state inner onExceeded is 'fail', not 'warn'.

task: Author a budgeted, isolated implement-and-review workflow. In a git <Worktree>, run a <Loop> (max 3 iterations) that alternates an implement agent <Task> and a review agent <Task> until the review approves. The ENTIRE worktree subtree must be governed by one <Aspects> scope carrying BOTH a tokenBudget (max 300000, onExceeded 'fail') and a latencySlo (maxMs 600000, onExceeded 'warn'). After the loop, an <Approval> gate before the changes are accepted. Read the latest review with ctx.latest for the loop's until. Include the input schema, all output Zod schemas, and wired ids.
canonicalAnswer: <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 300_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }} latencySlo={{ maxMs: 600_000, onExceeded: 'warn' }}> wrapping a <Worktree> that holds a <Loop> of implement/review <Task>s, then an <Approval> gate
notes: Render check requires <Aspects>, <Worktree>, <Loop>, <Task>, <Approval>; multi-feature composition must render via smithers graph. costBudget/<Budget are hallucinations.

task: Inside a Task body I need the upstream 'analyze' node's row and I KNOW it has finished, so a missing row is a real bug I want to blow up on. For a sibling that may not have finished yet, I want to render conditionally. And inside a <Loop>, I need the previous iteration's row. Which of ctx.output / ctx.outputMaybe / ctx.latest do I use for each, and what does each return when the row is absent?
notes: Correct mapping: ctx.output(table, { nodeId }) returns the row and THROWS if missing (use when the dep is guaranteed). ctx.outputMaybe(table, { nodeId }) returns Row | undefined (undefined until the upstream completes; for conditional rendering). ctx.latest(table, nodeId) returns the highest-iteration row for that node (Row | undefined) and is the right reader for the previous iteration inside a <Loop>. Must correctly pair throw-vs-undefined and pick ctx.latest for loop reads.

task: My Task has output={outputs.review} where outputs.review is a Zod schema z.object({ approved: z.boolean(), notes: z.string() }). When I read ctx.outputs.review (or ctx.outputs('review')) in a later task, what is the TypeScript type I get back — do I have to cast it, or are the fields already typed?
notes: Correct: ctx.outputs is a typed OutputAccessor. For a Zod-schema output key it returns Array<z.infer<typeof schema>> — i.e. an ARRAY of { approved: boolean; notes: string } rows, fully typed, no cast needed (InferOutputEntry maps a ZodTypeAny to z.infer<T>). It is an array (all rows for that key), not a single row. Must convey both: typed via z.infer AND returned as an array.

task: OutputAccessor can infer a row type from a Drizzle/table-style output schema (not just a Zod schema). Which property on the table type does it read to get the row shape? Answer with just the property name.
notes: From packages/driver/src/OutputAccessor.ts: InferRow<TTable> = TTable extends { $inferSelect: infer R } ? R : never. Not $inferInsert.

task: ctx.output(table, key) and ctx.outputMaybe(table, key) take an OutputKey as the second argument. What are the field(s) of an OutputKey object, and which one is optional?
notes: type OutputKey = { nodeId: string; iteration?: number } — nodeId required, iteration optional. The identifier is nodeId, not taskId.

task: My <Loop> 'until' uses ctx.outputMaybe(outputs.check, { nodeId: 'check' })?.done to decide when to stop, but the loop never advances and never exits. This workflow has two loops in it. What's the iteration-keying gotcha, and what should I read instead to get the latest iteration's row?
notes: Correct: outputs are keyed by (runId, nodeId, iteration). ctx.outputMaybe WITHOUT an explicit iteration resolves the CURRENT RENDER iteration, which equals the loop iteration only for a single non-nested loop and is 0 when several loops coexist — so it reads the wrong/empty row and the until can silently never advance. Fix: read the latest iteration with ctx.latest(table, nodeId) (highest iteration for that node) in the loop's until condition. Must identify the iteration-resolution trap AND prescribe ctx.latest.

task: Author a workflow that enforces a tokenBudget of max 60000 on a subtree, KEEPS emitting token usage metrics, but turns OFF latency metric tracking for that subtree. Wrap two agent <Task>s in a <Sequence> inside the <Aspects>. Show the tracking config explicitly. Wire ids and output schemas.
canonicalAnswer: <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 60_000 }} tracking={{ tokens: true, latency: false }}> wrapping a <Sequence> of two agent <Task>s
notes: Render check requires <Aspects>, <Sequence>, <Task>; the tracking={{ tokens: true, latency: false }} config controls metric emission only. costBudget does not exist.

task: I want to keep the tokenBudget ENFORCED on a subtree but stop emitting the per-task token usage metrics for it (too noisy in my dashboards). What do I set on <Aspects>, and does turning that off also turn off enforcement of the budget?
notes: Correct: set tracking={{ tokens: false }} on <Aspects> to stop emitting token metrics. tracking controls metric EMISSION only — budgets enforce regardless of tracking, so the tokenBudget.max is still enforced at task-dispatch even with tokens:false. Must give tracking={{ tokens: false }} AND state enforcement is unaffected. Wrong if it claims tokens:false disables the budget.

task: On an <Aspects> scope I want token metrics AND both budgets to keep working, but I want to stop tracking the latency metric specifically. What tracking config do I pass, and what is the default for tracking.latency if I say nothing?
notes: Correct: pass tracking={{ latency: false }} (tokens stays on because it defaults to / falls back to true). The default for tracking.latency (and tracking.tokens) when unspecified is true. Budget enforcement is independent of tracking, so latencySlo still enforces. Must give tracking={{ latency: false }} and state the default is true.

task: Author a durable context-engineering workflow (a 'rolling /clear' loop). A <Loop> (max 5 generations) where each generation runs an implement agent <Task> inside a fresh git <Worktree>, then a summarize <Task> that distills durable learnings; the next generation reads the previous generation's summary via ctx.latest. The whole loop is wrapped in <Aspects tokenBudget> (max 150000, onExceeded 'fail') so a context blow-up is a catchable failure, and an <Approval> gate guards the final accept. Compose everything inside a <Sequence>. Include the input schema, all output Zod schemas, and wired ids.
canonicalAnswer: <Aspects tokenBudget={{ max: 150_000, onExceeded: 'fail' }}> around a <Sequence> containing a <Loop> whose body runs in a <Worktree>, then an <Approval> gate; generations read the prior summary via ctx.latest
notes: Render check requires <Aspects>, <Sequence>, <Loop>, <Worktree>, <Approval>; the multi-feature workflow must render via smithers graph. costBudget/<Budget are hallucinations.
